feedback.text: In today's digital market, it is heavily sided in favor of the corporations, and isn't balanced properly. How so? To these corporations: - Our digital purchased goods aren't ours to keep (they sell a "license" to us which they can withdraw at any given time. With, or without, any reason they deem fit). - Purchases made on platforms like iTunes, Apple TV, Amazon, PlayStation (Sony), Qobuz, Ubisoft, Electronic Arts (just to name a few) can, will, and are being removed for any given reason. This effectively means that digital goods that are purchased doesn't belong to the end user, but rather to the corporations. This isn't how it should be. Once sold, the end user must be able to keep their purchases and fully enjoy them in perpetuity. - This problem concerns all facets of the entertainment industry: Movies, Music, Video Games. For example, Ubisoft has shut down The Crew (a game they sold to customers as a good), making it unplayable, and removed all digital copies from their customers' library while at the same time running a promotion on the newer game, The Crew 2, for 1 on all platforms. Another game will share The Crew's fate: Electronic Arts' game called "Anthem", another paid game that will be shut down on January 12, 2026. These games aren't the soles one... there are hundreds more, too many to list, and this grim list keeps on getting larger with every passing day. Amazon and Sony has removed movies and musics from their customers' library after Amazon's licence with various publishers ended. Customers should be able to enjoy their purchase fully without being worried about a licensing agreement between the one entity who sold the good to the customer, and that seller's licensor. - Payment processing companies like MasterCard and Visa can, will, and are actively bullying other companies like Steam and itch.io into not selling certain legal goods (by threatening to not process payment on perfectly legal transactions in they don't abide by arbitrary rules that puts them in a dire position when facing the competition, which will lead to financial ruin wether they submit to this attack on their business, thanks to MasterCard and Visa's duopolistic position, or not. MasterCard and Visa's bullying is a attack that is destroying what people in the E.U. and abroad are able to purchase and is a direct violation of the TFEU Article 102 / Digital Markets Act (Regulation (EU 2022/1925) E-Commerce Directive (2000/31/EC) / Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC) Fundamental Rights Actions (EU Charter Arts. 11 & 16). The European Commission needs to investigate these matters, to protect their citizens against the unfair practices that are led by the corporations. To protect their citizen's right to retain their purchased goods and enjoy them fully digitally. To curb MasterCard and Visa's duopolistic attitude toward legal buisinesses and make sure they do not intefere with legal purchases made by E.U. citizens.
